key_insight: The paper defines a “lattice gene” as a compact latent encoding of local Kikuchi diffraction patterns, and a “lattice genome” as their spatial map across a microstructure to capture crystalline heterogeneity at grain and subgrain scales.
engineering_relevance: This provides a data-driven way to quantify and visualize mesoscale structure in metals, helping link processing-induced microstructural heterogeneity to properties in additively manufactured and wrought alloys.
method: Encode EBSD Kikuchi patterns with a variational autoencoder to obtain a low-dimensional lattice gene.
Assemble spatially resolved lattice genes into a lattice genome across representative areas.
Use latent-space distances, angles, and spreads for segmentation and heterogeneity metrics.
evidence_signal: The approach is demonstrated on EBSD microstructures from additively manufactured and wrought Ni-base superalloys in both as-built and recrystallized states, showing latent maps, segmentation, and spread metrics.
watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and representativeness of EBSD data, and the latent-space metrics may require careful calibration to remain physically interpretable across different materials and scan conditions.
paper_title: Lattice genome: representation and analysis of heterogeneous crystalline microstructures
